speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three. This is Amy Godin, interviewing Lisa Murik. Today is November 10, 1995. We're located in Silver Spring, Maryland, USA. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Lisa, let's go back to the time that-- when you were in the ghetto. Could you describe a particular experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, One morning, they picked me and a couple more girls and a couple men, and they took us on a truck with a big bomb on it. And they took us outside the city. And the only thing through our minds at that time was, they're taking us and to blow us up with the bomb together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we got at outskirt, in the ghetto, they knew about it. I don't know how they knew about it. And everybody was worried about us. So when we got outside the city, the Germans told us, get off the truck. They got a man take off the bomb and put it on the field and told everybody to run away. And they blew up the bomb by themself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were so shocked, when they told us to go back on the truck, we couldn't move. One helped each other back on that truck. And when they brought us back to the city and they told us to go home and when we walked into the ghetto, everybody was dancing and singing, seeing us alive. They-- they were sure that we're not all coming back. I myself didn't believe it, that I'm going to see my parents, my sisters again. There was a real miracle that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was on the truck with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Germans, soldiers. There was a few more girls, and a few men.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they pick you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They just picked us out. When we went to sign up for work that morning, they came in that Judenrat, and they picked whoever they want. You couldn't say you're not going. They might shoot you or something, or whip you with a whip. So they picked, you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mentioned that you had to go sign up for work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you went in, and you didn't really sign. You just went in and you sat there in the room. You waited. And the Germans came in there, and they picked certain people and they took them to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any days that you didn't get picked to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, most every day, different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your whole family work as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just my two younger sisters. They were always inside the ghetto, staying behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents. And we never knew who was gonna come home and who was not gonna come home. And if you came home at night and everybody was there, you were very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What food did you eat when you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- I tell you truth, I don't remember even. It's so-- the only [INAUDIBLE] was hard. You hardly have any decent food I would say. No, if it was there, we would just go and [INAUDIBLE] about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other experiences that happened during the time that you were in the Ostrog ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That's the only other experience I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's move on to when you were in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the living situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that hole we were in was very small. It's not big. And we only had to sleep on was just plain straw, and that's it. And whatever we had on our bodies from clothes, that's all we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say the hole was five feet big?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. It's hard to judge it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very deep?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very deep.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ground. That's why probably kept us warm in there, because it was way under.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any blankets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Coats? Shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I had shoes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm, OK. Getting back to the night that you spent outside, could you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the place was, like I said, outskirts of the city. And was the woods around it where we were. And when we went out, we went into the woods. And we laid on the ice and snow all night. That's how we slept at night. You really didn't sleep. Because we got one next to each other so we kept warm. And we-- mostly, the night, we were up. Wait and see what's gonna happen the next morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any blankets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. Just, I-- I had one slip on me. And my father had a pair of slacks and a shirt. And nobody had much warm clothes at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any thoughts that went through your head that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thought was to be safe, to be alive. That's all you were thinking. Each day, you thank God that you woke up in the morning, you are alive. When you went to sleep, you are alive, and you get up the next morning. That's all. We were waiting, like, for the Messiah, we would say, to wait the day to come to be, you know, liberated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, when you were in hiding, do you remember any of the conversations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Did you have any light at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Light. We were all-- in the nighttime, when it came nighttime, it was dark. We couldn't have any light, because it would give us out. And we just were not worried about ourselves. We also worried for the woman who hid us. Because when the Germans find Jewish people in a Christian home, they just-- not just kill the Jewish people. They kill the Christian too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Did you get to know that family very well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we-- since we been there, we got to know very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. OK, what was she like? What was her name, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Horpina.

speaker: interviewer
text: Horpina.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very nice woman and very religious woman, I would say. Because she always kept on saying, God sent us to her and she has to save us, which we were really thankful later. After we got liberated, my mother really thanked her very much. Because when we got here to the United States, my parents worked, and they sent packages to her. And they kept writing letters till the day she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] How was it that you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the HIAS always arranged, for-- you know, looked out for people have families. And if people didn't have families, they brought over, anyway, people to the United States. Somehow, my husband [INAUDIBLE] because he had cousins here. He knew he had cousins. And the HIAS find them out. They were all living here in Washington at that time. And they made our papers. You had to make up papers when you brought somebody over that you have a job for those people to come here. And so one day, we got a letter that-- from his family, from the HIAS that they want us to come to the United States. So we left Germany by boat. We were on a boat two weeks. It was a cargo ship, we came here to the United States. We went through-- we came through New Orleans here. And my daughter at that time was 2 and 1/2. And we came here. And my husband's family got us a place to stay and my husband a job. And I didn't work right away when I got here. Only my husband worked. Then, after a while, his family got us a little business. And I couldn't hardly speak any English. And my daughter also hardly, you know, spoke any English at all. And they got us a little business. And the way we got known everything, when people came into the store, they picked up the item they wanted. And the items were marked, the prices on everything. That's how we started off our little business. And that's how it went on. And I learned the English language from the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came with you to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and my daughter. We came first. Then later on, my parents came with my other two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were your parents when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was-- we left them in Germany. And they came later on to-- they came to New York. My mother had family in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to a decision that you wanted to go to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: This I cannot remember. It's really blank.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When you came to the United States, where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cousins-- one of the cousin's wife lived by herself in a house with her daughter. So they put us up there first. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Washington. It was in Washington. So we lived there for a while with the woman and her daughter. Then, when my [BACKROUND NOISE] husband started working and making some money, we rented a room from another old lady. And we lived there for a while. Then, when we got the little business, we lived on top. On the store, there was living quarters. We lived there for a while, which was not such a great living quarters. And my-- my older son got born in that place. And after there, when we got ourselves on our feet, you know, real great, and we bought another business. And we lived also with the business together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of businesses were these?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grocery. And from there, in 1964, we move to Maryland. My daughter at that time had to go into college, my older daughter. So we moved into Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in the United States when?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you went to Washington, DC.

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight to Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- when we first came, where we lived, at the first place of living, there on that street was not too many Jewish people. And we didn't start really get together with Jewish people till later on. We joined the synagogue. And that's when we started meeting Jewish people. And then, my husband has a brother behind in Germany with a wife and a daughter also. And my husband, he brought them over here to the United States also. That's-- he was-- he and his brother were from seven children. They left two of them. And we were very close. And then, we met other people-- Holocaust survivors-- and we got to know each other. And that's how life went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did you-- when did you realize what happened during the Holocaust to so many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- we heard from other people, you know, from other Holocaust survivors how there were camps, you know, people into gas chambers, and all, you know, whatever happened at that time. And we really realized how lucky we were that we didn't ended up in the gas chambers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's probably where my father's and my mother's families landed up and died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever locate any of your other relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never could find them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did your parents come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in the '50s. They came through New York. And that's where they settled, New York City. I was the only one in Washington. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to Washington and had your business, describe an average day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you-- we can-- got up in the morning. And then, like any other person, had, you know, breakfast. And we went to the store and worked very hard. We didn't get home until 11 o'clock in the morning. We didn't have any days off at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked on the Shabbos.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you-- we worked on Shabbat, because we had to make a living. We had to put ourselves on our feet. And it was very hard. And we worked till the riot broke out, 1968. We were in business till then. Then when the riot came, our business was destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What riot was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- when they killed Martin Luther King. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, we gave up the business. And my husband retired. Now, he worked part-time, because you have to make a living. And then, that's how I went on till he passed away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, did people ask you about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first start talking to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the Holocaust? I didn't-- you know, I spoke to-- you know, in the family. You know, we were talking about it. Then after my daughter got married and her oldest child-- my grandson, Jeff-- was born and he got in already high school. He was interested. And he wanted to make a tape of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened at that time I got a-- that was 1989-- I had a call from the Washington Museum and to-- if I would like to come to be interviewed. And I-- at first, I was thinking, maybe not then. I says, well, what can hurt? Let's do it. And I gave my story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the tape-- well, really, which was a little easier to me that time. The person who interviewed me was-- was my grandson's best friend, and they went to school together. So it was a little easy. And then, I, you know, was trying to keep the best. They only interviewed me for the part of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your children. You mentioned that you had one--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my daughter, Barbara, my son, Sam, and my son, Charles-- my younger one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you mentioned grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have four grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jeff, Erica, Michelle, and Shawna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your parents still living?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my parents passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died in 1969. And my father passed away in 1983.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters live in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both of them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my brother lived here in Maryland. And he just moved away to Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a very close relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. We're very close. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences have affected the way that you have brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a little over-protective, I would say. They don't-- [BACKROUND NOISE] especially my grandson, he-- when I used to tell him, you know, don't go here, do that. They call me Bubby. And they-- he used to say, Bubby, don't worry. I'll be fine. I'll be fine. And I used to tell him, the only way I'm so protective over them because what I went through in my life. You think something can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they know about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A lot of people saw my tape. I've been taking to schools and showing them. And the school where my-- where Jeff and Erica goes to, I used to go and show them the tape, and a lot of time I spoke on the anniversary of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Are there any other experiences that you would like to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: From here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm, I belong to a synagogue. I do volunteer work in the synagogue. I'm very committed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of that synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beth Sholom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Potomac, Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living in the Washington area and had your business, did you experience any anti-Semitism at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, people were very nice to us. I don't know-- because we were from a different country or-- no, they were very nice. And everything was a normal, you know, time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any messages that you would like to give to your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just want them to be good people and do good deeds. That's the only-- and just try to follow my footsteps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's here. She might want--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we have your daughter with us now. Could you introduce her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my daughter, Barbara Chesman. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi. I just wanted to express my appreciation that this is a-- a really wonderful project that Mr. Spielberg has undertaken. And I think it's great that all these stories will be available for anyone that wants to hear them. And they should hear them. I feel very fortunate myself that I'm here today. I-- I was born in a DP camp in Berlin right after the war. And I think my mother's story is-- is one of those unique stories. It's-- it's much different than having been in a concentration camp or some of the other stories that I've heard and seen. But it's a very unbelievable story. And I think that if-- the more people that hear and-- and understand these things, then maybe these kind of things will never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on the right, sitting down, is my father. Standin' up next to him is my mother. On the left, is my-- young-- my middle sister, Edith. And in the middle, it's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your parents' names?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give your mother's full name and your father's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is [? Gossie ?] [? Terby. ?] My father's Elias. And I'm Lisa in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And  where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in my hometown, Ostrog, maybe-- [SIGHS] I don't exactly remember the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately what year it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was five then. So 1930, probably. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, and that was taken in September, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my sister, Edith. And on the left is me, Lisa. And that was taken in Lodz in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a reception after my wedding. On the right, is my mother, [? Gossie ?] [? Terby. ?] I am next, Lisa. Next to me is my husband, Abraham. And next to Abraham is my father, Elias. And the rest of the people, I can't remember their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken 1945 in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my mother, [? Gossie ?] [? Terby, ?] on the left and my father, Elias, on the right. This was-- this was taken in a park in-- in Israel in 1968.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture, on the right is my husband, Abraham. On the left, is me, Lisa. This was taken at the house in Maryland, [SIGHS] 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let's see-- 1972, I think. not positive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my niece, Esther. And on the left is my husband, Abraham. And this was taken in 1985. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Perfect.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right is my nephew, Morris. On the left is me, Lisa. And on Morris' lap is sitting his daughter, Laura. And I am holding the baby, Jordan. And that was taken 1986.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in here, in Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this picture was taken at a synagogue [? Kodesh ?] in 1993. It was my granddaughter Erica's bat mitzvah. And the front row on the left is my granddaughter, Michelle. On the right, next to her is my granddaughter, Shawna. The second row on the right is my son, Charles. Next to him, my granddaughter, Erica. Next to Erica is me, Lisa. And next to me is my daughter-in-law, Alyse. The third row on the right is my handsome grandson, Jeff. Next to him, his mother, Barbara. Next to Barbara is her husband, Bernie. And next to Bernie on the left is my son, Sam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 19-- October, 1994 in Jerusalem, Israel. It was my niece's wedding. On the right, in the front row is my brother, Mike. Next to him, his son-in-law, David. Next to David on the left, his wife, Esther. And next to Esther is her mother-- what's her name? Shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go in the back. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's her mother. In the back row on the right is me, Lisa. Next to me is a cousin, Manya. Next to Manya is Rochelle. Next to Rochelle. is my sister, Anne. Next to Anne, to the left is my nephew, Ira. Next to Ira is my nephew, Todd. And next to Todd is Gary.
